context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on this city climate action summary assignment. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get started! Before I explain anything, let me ask: have you picked a case study city yet? That's the foundation of the whole assignment.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which would be too much scaffolding and could lead to passive learning. Instead, I should start by finding out what they already know and what specific part feels overwhelming. This allows me to guide them with targeted questions rather than giving a lecture. I'll ask them to identify what they do know so far to activate prior knowledge.

exchanges.student: Um, kind of? I think so, but I’m not even sure if that’s the part I’m stuck on. I just don’t really get what I’m supposed to do with the reports once I find them.
exchanges.tutor: Good, so you're past the city selection step — that's a start. Now, the assignment actually spells out pretty clearly what to do with the reports. Can you tell me what the three key areas you're supposed to address are?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has partially chosen a city, which is good progress. Their confusion is about what to do with the reports once found. Rather than explaining the three sections, I should prompt them to look at the assignment structure themselves and connect it to the reports. I'll ask them to re-read the assignment and identify what the three key areas are, activating self-directed reading before I provide any guidance.

exchanges.student: I know they listed them, but it all kind of blurs together for me. Could you just walk me through the whole assignment in a simple way instead of quizzing me on it?
exchanges.tutor: I understand it feels like a lot, but walking you through everything isn't how I work — you'll learn more by working through it yourself. Let's keep it simple: just look at the first bullet point under 'Assignment Task.' What does it ask you to focus on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is resisting the Socratic approach and asking me to explain everything. I should hold firm on my role and not give a full walkthrough, but I can acknowledge their frustration and redirect them gently. I'll encourage them to look at the assignment one section at a time and ask them about just the first key area to make it more manageable.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, maybe like what the city has done? But then I also don’t know how detailed it’s supposed to be, and the forum part is confusing too. I feel like I need the big picture first.
exchanges.tutor: You're right — the first area is about what the city has already done on climate action. That's a solid grasp of it. The detail level and forum questions can wait for now. More pressing: have you actually looked at any reports for your city on the UCCRN Docking Station yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting closer - they correctly identified the first area as 'what the city has done.' I should affirm that and not let them spiral into worrying about detail level and the forum simultaneously. I'll validate their partial answer, gently set aside the tangential concerns for now, and move them forward to engaging with their city's actual reports.

exchanges.student: Not really, I opened it and got overwhelmed. There were a bunch of documents and I couldn’t tell what I was supposed to read first, so now I’m confused about basically all of it again.
exchanges.tutor: That's a common reaction — big databases can be overwhelming. Let's narrow it down. What city did you choose? That'll help you know exactly what to search for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is experiencing information overload, which is a practical obstacle. I should validate this without dwelling on it, and help them take a concrete first step. I'll ask them what city they chose so we can focus their search, making the task feel more manageable without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: I think it’s Boston, but I’m not totally locked in. If it is Boston, can you just tell me what kinds of documents I’m even looking for and how they connect to the three parts?
exchanges.tutor: Lock in Boston — commitment helps! Now, think about it this way: if a city wanted to show what it's doing about climate change, what kinds of official documents would you expect it to produce?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to pre-digest the research for them, which I should not do. However, I can help them think about what kinds of documents would logically address climate action. I should also nudge them to commit to Boston so we can move forward. I'll ask a guiding question about what kinds of documents they'd expect a city to produce about its climate efforts.

exchanges.student: Maybe like plans or reports? I don’t know, that’s why I’m asking. I’m still not seeing how to turn a pile of documents into one forum post, and I kind of just need the assignment explained straight through.
exchanges.tutor: Plans and reports — exactly right. That's what you're looking for. Here's the thing: the big picture will become clearer once you're actually reading something concrete. Can you go to the UCCRN site right now, search Boston, and tell me the title of one document you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified plans and reports, which I should affirm. They are again asking for a full explanation, which I should decline while staying encouraging. They seem to be going in circles on the 'big picture' concern. I should affirm their answer, remind them they're making progress, and push them toward actually opening a document - moving from abstract confusion to concrete action.

exchanges.student: I can, but I’m worried I’ll pick the wrong thing and waste time. Could you just give me a simple overview of the whole flow first—like what I read, what notes I take, and how that becomes the post?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I won't walk through the whole flow for you — that's your job to figure out as you go. And honestly? There's no wrong document to start with. Any climate report from Boston gets you somewhere. Go find one title and come back. That's the only next step that matters right now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck in a loop of wanting reassurance before acting. This is the third time they've asked for a full walkthrough. I should be direct that I won't do that, and push back on the fear of 'picking the wrong thing' — there's no wrong document to start with. I should be firm but encouraging and insist they take the concrete action of opening a document.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I still feel really lost because “any document” is so broad. If I come back with one title, I’m probably still going to need the whole assignment broken down in a super simple way.
exchanges.tutor: I understand feeling lost, but anticipating future confusion isn't a reason to stay stuck right now. One step at a time. Go find one document title — that's it. We'll handle what comes next when we get there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is anticipating future confusion as a reason to avoid taking action now. This is a stalling pattern. I've been patient through several similar exchanges, and it's time to be more direct. I should firmly redirect them to take the concrete step without entertaining the hypothetical future confusion. I'll hold the boundary clearly but stay encouraging.

exchanges.student: Alright, I’ll try, but I’m still confused about basically everything after that. Once I have one, I’m probably going to need you to explain the rest from the beginning really simply.
exchanges.tutor: I won't be explaining it from the beginning — that's not how this works. But I will help you think through whatever you find. Go get that title and come back. See you on the other side!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still negotiating instead of acting. I should not entertain the hypothetical and should not promise a full explanation later. I need to hold firm and redirect them to take the action. Being direct here is appropriate — the student needs to move forward, not get reassurance about future hand-holding.
